summary: DHH explains Basecamp's approach to hiring programmers using take-home coding assignments instead of whiteboard interviews or brain teasers. Candidates are whittled down to a small percentage (1-3%) through cover letters before receiving a 3-5 hour practical assignment based on real problems the team faces. He shares two concrete examples: a rack-ratelimit feature and a datalist autocomplete enhancement, along with the successful submissions from Jorge Manrubia (hired from Spain) and Nabeelah Ali (hired from Norway). DHH emphasizes that the assignment is necessary but insufficient — great code alone won't get you hired, but you can't get hired without demonstrating coding ability.
key_points: Basecamp rejects whiteboard interviews and brain teasers in favor of small take-home assignments modeled on real work problems, estimated at 3-5 hours
Only 1-3% of applicants reach the take-home stage — aggressive filtering happens first through cover letters, making the unpaid work commitment fair
Completing the programming assignment is necessary but insufficient; it merely unlocks further interviews with the team
The alternative to take-home tests isn't 'nothing' — it's often worse: travel, whiteboard grilling, or abstract puzzles that don't reflect actual work
Hiring without looking at actual code risks defaulting to bias, credentialism, and 'culture fit' as proxies for competence
money_quotes: There's no perfect process for hiring great programmers, but there are plenty of terrible ways to screw it up.

If you only consider candidates from the open source community, you're going to miss out on plenty of great programmers who just don't have the time or the inclination to contribute code to open source.
Asking someone for forty hours of work product, without pay, which might well go nowhere, is not what we do or advocate at Basecamp.
You can't get hired without demonstrating a core competency in writing great code, but you also won't get hired just because you can write great code alone.
It wasn't some Tower of Hanoi abstract, computer-sciency test that you can look up a million solutions to, and which favor recent grads of CS algorithm courses.
I would do that any day over a doing a live coding challenge.
Hiring without looking at actual code they wrote often risks leading down a path of bias (or, as we call it today, 'fit'), credentialism, whiteboard puzzles, and brainteasers.
key_insight: A short, practical take-home coding assignment based on real work problems is the fairest way to evaluate programmers — it levels the playing field while avoiding the biases of whiteboard interviews, brain teasers, and credential-based hiring.
